summary: Shapiro follows up on his coffee table project that embeds slate tiles into wood. He describes the finishing process using Minwax Polycrylic, noting the care needed to avoid bubbles on rough slate surfaces. He recounts a mistake of waxing the tiles, which caused buildup in crevices that made them look dirty. After removing the wax manually, he concludes the tiles work great as drink coasters and recommends the technique for future projects.
key_points: Rough natural slate tiles need careful brushing with polyurethane to avoid bubbles in the finish
Waxing sealed slate tiles was a mistake — wax accumulated in crevices and couldn't be chemically removed without risking the surrounding shellac
Despite the finishing misstep, embedded slate tiles proved durable and functional as built-in coasters
Sometimes the simplest fix (elbow grease) is the only safe option when chemical solvents would cause collateral damage
money_quotes: Unfortunately, I then decided to get clever.
The wax built up in the crevices and was very noticable. It made the things look dirty.
Unfortunately, anything that would have dissolved the wax would also dissolve the nearby shellac finish and I didn't want to take any chances.
I used good old elbow grease to get rid of the wax.
They don't ring when you put drinks on them (their main purpose in life).
Try out some tiles on your next project. You'll thank me for it.
key_insight: Embedded slate tiles in wood furniture work beautifully as built-in coasters, but resist the urge to over-finish them — getting clever with extra coatings can create more problems than it solves.
